title: 04 - Hemodynamic Disorders, Thromboembolism, and Shock - Flashcards
description: Third-party generated Chapter 4 flashcards optimized for active recall, spaced repetition, and exam revision. The deck follows the main syllabus-aligned chapter buckets and prioritizes high-yield mechanisms, distinctions, morphology, and clinical correlations from Robbins Basic Pathology, 10th Edition, Chapter 4.

disclaimer.endorsement_notice: These flashcards are third-party generated study materials. They are not produced by, reviewed by, approved by, endorsed by, or affiliated with the textbook authors, Elsevier, the Royal College of Pathologists of Australasia, or any other authority, institution, publisher, or examining body.
disclaimer.use_note: Use these flashcards as revision support alongside the original source material.

source_notes.source_title: Robbins Basic Pathology, 10th Edition, Chapter 4: Hemodynamic Disorders, Thromboembolism, and Shock
RCPA - Basic Pathological Sciences Syllabus 2026 - October 2025
source_notes.organization: Royal College of Pathologists of Australasia
source_notes.reference_note: Used for topic emphasis, exam framing, and laboratory-test emphasis.

source_notes.source_title: Chapter 4 Study Notes and Exam Prep Questions created from the approved Chapter 4 source set
source_notes.reference_note: Used to compress Chapter 4 into single-fact active-recall prompts.

deck.deck_name: Pathology::Chapter 04 - Hemodynamic Disorders, Thromboembolism, and Shock
deck.design_note: Cards are kept mostly single-fact and under one question per card for rapid recall.
deck.page_reference_note: The source_page_refs arrays use the printed book page number from Robbins Basic Pathology 10th Edition, not the sequential PDF page position. Chapter 4 occupies book pages 97-119.

cards.front: What is hyperemia?
cards.back: An active increase in tissue blood volume caused by arteriolar dilation and increased inflow.

cards.front: What is congestion?
cards.back: A passive increase in tissue blood volume caused by impaired venous outflow.

cards.front: Why do congested tissues look blue-red?
cards.back: Because deoxygenated hemoglobin accumulates in the affected area.

cards.front: What are heart failure cells?
cards.back: Hemosiderin-laden alveolar macrophages seen in chronic pulmonary congestion.

cards.front: What gross liver appearance is classic for chronic passive congestion?
cards.back: Nutmeg liver, with red-brown centrilobular areas against paler surrounding parenchyma.

cards.front: What is edema?
cards.back: Accumulation of excess interstitial fluid within tissues.

cards.front: What term describes severe generalized edema with marked swelling and cavity fluid accumulation?
cards.back: Anasarca.

cards.front: Which two opposing forces mainly govern fluid movement between vascular and interstitial spaces?
cards.back: Hydrostatic pressure and plasma colloid osmotic pressure.

cards.front: What type of edema fluid is expected when hydrostatic pressure rises or oncotic pressure falls?
cards.back: A protein-poor transudate.

cards.front: What type of edema fluid forms when vascular permeability increases in inflammation?
cards.back: A protein-rich exudate.

cards.front: What is the most common systemic cause of edema due to increased hydrostatic pressure?
cards.back: Congestive heart failure.

cards.front: Why does heart failure often worsen edema through the kidney?
cards.back: Reduced renal perfusion activates the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system, causing sodium and water retention.

cards.front: Which plasma protein is the main determinant of plasma colloid osmotic pressure in edema discussions?
cards.back: Albumin.

cards.front: What is the mechanism of arm lymphedema after axillary node dissection or irradiation?
cards.back: Lymphatic obstruction with impaired resorption of interstitial fluid.

cards.front: What is peau d'orange in breast pathology caused by?
cards.back: Edema of overlying skin due to obstruction of superficial lymphatics, often by breast carcinoma.

cards.front: Where is dependent edema most prominent in a standing patient?
cards.back: In the legs, because hydrostatic pressure is greatest there.

cards.front: Periorbital edema is especially suggestive of what underlying problem?
cards.back: Renal dysfunction or nephrotic syndrome.

cards.front: Why can pulmonary edema be rapidly fatal?
cards.back: It impairs oxygen diffusion and creates a favorable setting for infection.

cards.front: What size are petechiae?
cards.back: Minute hemorrhages about 1 to 2 mm in diameter.

cards.front: What size are purpura?
cards.back: Slightly larger hemorrhages, about 3 to 5 mm.

cards.front: What size are ecchymoses?
cards.back: Larger subcutaneous hematomas about 1 to 2 cm in size.

cards.front: What causes the color change of a bruise over time?
cards.back: Enzymatic conversion of hemoglobin to bilirubin and then hemosiderin within macrophages.

cards.front: What is the earliest immediate response of an arteriole to vascular injury in hemostasis?
cards.back: Transient vasoconstriction.

cards.front: Which platelet receptor binds vWF during initial platelet adhesion?
cards.back: GpIb.

cards.front: What molecule bridges platelet GpIb to exposed collagen?
cards.back: von Willebrand factor.

cards.front: Which platelet granules contain ADP and calcium?
cards.back: Dense granules.

cards.front: Name one major coagulation-related protein stored in platelet alpha granules.
cards.back: Examples include fibrinogen, factor V, and von Willebrand factor.

cards.front: Why does aspirin impair platelet aggregation?
cards.back: It inhibits platelet cyclooxygenase and reduces thromboxane A2 synthesis.

cards.front: Which platelet receptor binds fibrinogen during platelet aggregation?
cards.back: GpIIb/IIIa.

cards.front: What is the major initiator of coagulation in vivo?
cards.back: Tissue factor acting with factor VIIa at the site of injury.

cards.front: Which pathway is screened by the prothrombin time test?
cards.back: The extrinsic and common pathway, especially factor VII plus X, V, II, and fibrinogen.

cards.front: Which pathway is screened by the partial thromboplastin time test?
cards.back: The intrinsic and common pathway, including XII, XI, IX, VIII, X, V, II, and fibrinogen.

cards.front: What is the central hemostatic action of thrombin?
cards.back: It converts fibrinogen to fibrin and amplifies coagulation by activating other factors and platelets.

cards.front: What does an elevated D-dimer indicate?
cards.back: Breakdown of cross-linked fibrin, suggesting active thrombosis and fibrinolysis.

cards.front: What does thrombomodulin do in hemostasis?
cards.back: It binds thrombin on intact endothelium and converts it into an activator of protein C.

cards.front: Which endothelial mediators directly inhibit platelet activation and aggregation?
cards.back: Prostacyclin (PGI2), nitric oxide, and adenosine diphosphatase.

cards.front: What are the three components of Virchow triad?
cards.back: Endothelial injury, abnormal blood flow, and hypercoagulability.

cards.front: Why does stasis favor venous thrombosis?
cards.back: It reduces washout of activated clotting factors, limits inflow of inhibitors, and lets platelets and leukocytes contact endothelium.

cards.front: What is the key defect in factor V Leiden?
cards.back: Factor V becomes resistant to proteolysis by activated protein C.

cards.front: What is the effect of the prothrombin G20210A variant?
cards.back: It increases prothrombin transcription and raises the risk of venous thrombosis.

cards.front: Which natural anticoagulant deficiencies are classic inherited causes of recurrent venous thrombosis?
cards.back: Antithrombin III deficiency, protein C deficiency, and protein S deficiency.

cards.front: What causes heparin-induced thrombocytopenia?
cards.back: Autoantibodies against complexes of heparin and platelet factor 4 that trigger platelet activation and thrombosis.

cards.front: What reproductive clue strongly suggests antiphospholipid antibody syndrome?
cards.back: Repeated miscarriages or fetal loss.

cards.front: What do lines of Zahn indicate?
cards.back: That a thrombus formed in flowing blood before death.

cards.front: Are arterial thrombi typically platelet-rich or red-cell-rich?
cards.back: Platelet-rich.

cards.front: Why are venous thrombi often called red or stasis thrombi?
cards.back: Because they form in sluggish venous blood flow and contain many enmeshed red cells.

cards.front: What does organization and recanalization mean for an older thrombus?
cards.back: It is invaded by cells and connective tissue, and new channels may form that partially restore flow.

cards.front: Where do most venous thrombi occur?
cards.back: In the veins of the lower extremities.

cards.front: What is disseminated intravascular coagulation in one sentence?
cards.back: Widespread microvascular thrombosis that consumes platelets and clotting proteins while fibrinolysis is also activated.

cards.front: What is an embolus?
cards.back: A detached intravascular solid, liquid, or gaseous mass carried by blood to a distant site.

cards.front: Where do most pulmonary thromboemboli originate?
cards.back: From deep venous thromboses in leg veins proximal to the popliteal fossa.

cards.front: What is the classic danger of a large saddle pulmonary embolus?
cards.back: Sudden death from obstruction at the bifurcation of the main pulmonary arteries.

cards.front: What is the most common source of systemic arterial emboli?
cards.back: Intracardiac mural thrombi, especially after left ventricular infarction or from dilated left atria.

cards.front: What clinical triad helps recognize fat embolism syndrome after major skeletal trauma?
cards.back: Pulmonary insufficiency, neurologic symptoms, and a petechial rash, often with anemia and thrombocytopenia.

cards.front: What presentation is typical of amniotic fluid embolism?
cards.back: Sudden severe dyspnea, cyanosis, hypotensive shock, seizures or coma, often followed by pulmonary edema and DIC.

cards.front: What causes decompression sickness?
cards.back: Rapid pressure reduction allows dissolved nitrogen to bubble out of blood and tissues as gas emboli.

cards.front: When do red infarcts typically occur?
cards.back: With venous occlusion, in loose tissues, in tissues with dual circulation, or after reperfusion of previously ischemic tissue.

cards.front: When do white infarcts typically occur?
cards.back: With arterial occlusion in solid organs that have end-arterial circulations, such as heart, spleen, and kidney.

cards.front: Which cells are most rapidly irreversibly injured by ischemia?
cards.back: Neurons.

cards.front: What is shock?
cards.back: A state of systemic tissue hypoperfusion caused by reduced cardiac output or reduced effective circulating blood volume.

cards.front: What are the three major categories of shock in this chapter?
cards.back: Cardiogenic, hypovolemic, and septic shock.

cards.front: Which organisms most commonly trigger septic shock according to this chapter?
cards.back: Most often gram-positive bacteria, followed by gram-negative bacteria and fungi.

cards.front: Which laboratory marker is specifically noted as clinically useful in septic shock?
cards.back: Procalcitonin.

cards.front: What endothelial mediator contributes importantly to systemic vasodilation in septic shock?
cards.back: Nitric oxide.

cards.front: Why does septic shock often progress to DIC?
cards.back: Because inflammation drives tissue factor expression, suppresses anticoagulant pathways, and increases plasminogen activator inhibitor-1, favoring widespread microvascular thrombosis.

cards.front: Why does lactate rise in severe shock?
cards.back: Tissue hypoperfusion drives anaerobic glycolysis and lactic acidosis.

cards.front: What are the three classic stages of shock?
cards.back: Nonprogressive, progressive, and irreversible.

cards.front: What skin finding can distinguish early septic shock from most hypovolemic or cardiogenic shock states?
cards.back: Warm, flushed skin due to peripheral vasodilation.
